content.text: Typography properties control how text is rendered — its size, weight, spacing, family, and decoration. Good typographic CSS creates readable, hierarchical content that works across screen sizes. This document covers the core font and text properties, how to load custom fonts, and how to set up a coherent type system in an Angular project.

attrs.alt: CSS Typography: type scale, line-height anatomy, system font stacks, font-weight values, and text utility properties

content.text: property accepts a comma-separated list of font names. The browser uses the first one available. Always end with a generic family (
) render each OS's native UI font — fast, familiar, zero network requests. Modern design systems often start here before introducing a custom typeface.
Font size can be set with any length unit. The base (
respects the user's browser font-size preference;
overrides it. If a user has set their browser to 20px for accessibility,
Font weight controls the thickness of glyphs. Numeric values from 100 to 900 in steps of 100 map to named weights:
Not every weight is available in every font — the browser synthesizes missing weights, often poorly. Check the font's available weights before using non-standard values.
uses a separate font face with redesigned glyphs.
mechanically slants the upright face. Prefer italic when the font has a true italic variant.
sets the height of each line box. The difference between
becomes the leading — space added above and below the glyphs:

/* Unitless (recommended) — multiplied by element's font-size */
body { line-height: 1.6; }    /* body text — airy */
h1   { line-height: 1.15; }   /* headings — tight */
pre  { line-height: 1.4; }    /* code — moderate */

/* With units (not recommended — doesn't scale with font-size) */
p { line-height: 24px; }  /* avoid */
p { line-height: 150%; }  /* avoid — computed once at declaration time */
, the line height becomes fixed and compound components may look broken.
shorthand combines multiple properties in a specific order:
Large uppercase text benefits from positive
). Large display headings often benefit from slight negative tracking (
at the default — the font's built-in kerning is optimized for reading.

.nav-link { text-transform: uppercase; }    /* CSS uppercases the rendered text */
.name     { text-transform: capitalize; }   /* First letter of each word */
.reset    { text-transform: none; }         /* Override inherited transform */
over typing uppercase in HTML — the original casing is preserved for accessibility tools and copy-paste.
These properties control how text wraps and what happens when it overflows:
Preserves whitespace and newlines; no wrapping
Preserves whitespace; wraps at container edge
Breaks long words to prevent overflow
Breaks at any character (e.g. URLs, CJK text)
Modern CSS adds two intelligent wrapping modes that improve heading and paragraph appearance:

distributes characters more evenly across lines — a heading like "Introducing a New Way to Learn" won't end with a single word on the last line. It's a layout quality improvement with no negative effects.
is similar but optimised for body paragraphs. Both are supported in Chrome 114+ and Safari 17.4+.
shows the fallback font immediately while the custom font downloads, then swaps once ready. This prevents invisible text during font load (FOIT). The alternatives are
(skip swap if font isn't cached — best for non-critical decorative fonts).

<!-- index.html -->
<head>
  <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com" />
  <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.gstatic.com" crossorigin />
  <link
    href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Inter:wght@400;500;600;700&display=swap"
    rel="stylesheet"
  />
</head>

creates font sizes that scale with the viewport without needing breakpoints:
offset ensures the text doesn't collapse too small at narrow viewports. Use a fluid type scale generator (like utopia.fyi) to calculate these values precisely.
Accessibility: Minimum Font Size and Readability
— browsers default to 16px for a reason. Smaller text (under 14px) fails WCAG 1.4.4 for users who have set larger defaults.
— WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 for body text, 3:1 for large text (≥18pt).
— improves justified text and narrow columns without JavaScript.
Don't use `user-select: none` on readable text
— users may need to copy-paste content.
with no fallback renders system default if the font doesn't load. Always include a fallback chain.
— loading 8 weight variants of a font adds up to several hundred KB. Load only what you use: typically 400, 500/600, and 700.
— invisible text during font load (FOIT) creates a flash. Add
— letter-spacing is a design choice for large display text and uppercase labels. At body size it hurts readability.
— justified text creates uneven word spacing ('rivers') in narrow columns and harms readability for dyslexic users.

for uppercase labels and large headings; leave body text at default tracking.
